vowels.t: Short open back vowel. Very common in Kanien'kéha words. Do not add an English r-colour after it. IPA: [a].
The colon (:) always marks vowel length. A long vowel can completely change a word's meaning — never shorten it. IPA: [aː].
The acute accent marks primary stress. á is louder and higher in pitch than plain a. Stress can change grammatical meaning in Kanien'kéha. IPA: [á].

standard.r: á — stressed a — higher pitch, louder

eastern.r: á — stressed a — higher pitch, louder

ohsweken.r: á — stressed a — higher pitch, louder

vowels.t: The grave accent marks a falling tone — the pitch starts higher and drops through the vowel. It appears in specific grammatical contexts. IPA: [à].

standard.r: à — falling tone a — pitch drops

eastern.r: à — falling tone a — pitch drops

ohsweken.r: à — falling tone a — pitch drops

vowels.t: Short mid front vowel. Keep it pure — no gliding into 'ay' as in English. IPA: [e].
Held roughly twice as long as the short version. IPA: [eː].
Stressed e — louder and higher in pitch than plain e. IPA: [é].

standard.r: é — stressed e — higher pitch, louder

eastern.r: é — stressed e — higher pitch, louder

ohsweken.r: é — stressed e — higher pitch, louder

vowels.t: Falling tone e — pitch starts higher and drops. IPA: [è].

standard.r: è — falling tone e — pitch drops

eastern.r: è — falling tone e — pitch drops

ohsweken.r: è — falling tone e — pitch drops

vowels.t: High front vowel, like Spanish 'i'. Short and crisp. In Ontario dialects (Six Nations, Tyendinaga), some positions use 'y' where Kanehsatà:ke uses 'i' — both are valid. IPA: [i].
Stressed i — louder and higher in pitch than plain i. IPA: [í].

standard.r: í — stressed i — higher pitch, louder

eastern.r: í — stressed i — higher pitch, louder

ohsweken.r: í — stressed i — higher pitch, louder

vowels.t: Falling tone i — pitch starts higher and drops. IPA: [ì].

standard.r: ì — falling tone i — pitch drops

eastern.r: ì — falling tone i — pitch drops

ohsweken.r: ì — falling tone i — pitch drops

vowels.t: Rounded back vowel. Short unless marked with :. IPA: [o].
Length here distinguishes verb forms and possession. Always honour the colon. IPA: [oː].
Stressed o — louder and higher in pitch than plain o. IPA: [ó].

standard.r: ó — stressed o — higher pitch, louder

eastern.r: ó — stressed o — higher pitch, louder

ohsweken.r: ó — stressed o — higher pitch, louder

vowels.t: Falling tone o — pitch starts higher and drops. IPA: [ò].

standard.r: ò — falling tone o — pitch drops

eastern.r: ò — falling tone o — pitch drops

ohsweken.r: ò — falling tone o — pitch drops

vowels.t: Air flows through both mouth and nose simultaneously. Nasal vowels are a key feature of Iroquoian languages. The TTS approximation uses "an" but the n should not be heard as a separate consonant — it just signals nasalization. IPA: [ã].
Nasalized rounded back vowel. ę [ã] and ǫ [õ] are distinct — both common in Kanehsatà:ke Kanien'kéha. IPA: [õ].

consonants.t: Unaspirated velar stop — no puff of air after it, unlike English 'key'. Think of the 'k' in 'sky' or 'ski'. IPA: [k].

standard.r: k — unaspirated, like k in sky

eastern.r: k — unaspirated, like k in sky

ohsweken.r: k — written k, sounds like g in gate

consonants.t: Unaspirated alveolar stop. The 't' in 'stop', not 'top'. IPA: [t].

standard.r: t — unaspirated, like t in stop

eastern.r: t — unaspirated, like t in stop

ohsweken.r: t — before consonant; like d in dog before vowel/y

consonants.t: A full consonant in Kanien'kéha, not just punctuation. The throat closes briefly — exactly like the pause in English 'uh-oh'. It appears word-initially, mid-word, and word-finally, and changes meaning. IPA: [ʔ].
Glottal fricative. Breathy onset before vowels; can also appear between vowels. IPA: [h].
Standard alveolar nasal consonant, similar to English. IPA: [n].
Alveolar flap [ɾ] in Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke; lateral [l] in Akwesasne. Both are valid community-level dialect features, not errors. IPA: [ɾ] or [l].

consonants.ipaNote: alveolar fricative; voiced or palatal by context
consonants.s: like 's' in say — varies by context
consonants.t: Three pronunciations: like 's' in say before a consonant or vowel; like soft 'z' before a long stressed vowel; like 'sh' in she before y or i (dialect dependent). IPA: [s] / [z] / [ʃ].

ohsweken.r: s / z (before long stress) / sh (before y/i)

consonants.t: Labial-velar approximant. Smooth glide into the following vowel, similar to English. IPA: [w].

consonants.s: palatal glide; y in Ontario, i in Quebec
consonants.t: Palatal approximant [j] in Six Nations/Tyendinaga; high front vocoid [i] in Kanehsatà:ke/Kahnawà:ke in the same positions. Both are correct dialect forms. IPA: [j] or [i].
Alveolar affricate — a stop immediately followed by a fricative. Particularly consistent in Kahnawà:ke. IPA: [ts].
Palato-alveolar affricate, like English 'ch' in 'church'. IPA: [tʃ].

combinations.t: The hw combination — a voiceless w sound. Like 'wh' in 'where' in dialects that distinguish wh/w. IPA: [ʍ].
Aspirated k — a puff of air follows the stop, like 'c' in 'cat' or 'cow'. Contrast with plain k (unaspirated). IPA: [kʰ].
Aspirated kw — like 'qu' in 'queen' or 'quiet'. Combines the aspirated k with a simultaneous w rounding. IPA: [kʷʰ].

combinations.s: g in gate + w in way
combinations.t: Unaspirated kw — the g sound from 'gate' immediately followed by the w from 'way'. IPA: [kʷ].

combinations.s: g in gate + y in yet
combinations.t: Palatalised k — the g sound from 'gate' with the y from 'yet'. IPA: [kʲ].

combinations.s: n in now + y in yet
combinations.t: Palatalised n — the n from 'now' immediately followed by the y from 'yet'. IPA: [nʲ].
Palatalised r — the r/l flap followed by the y from 'yet'. IPA: [ɾʲ].

combinations.s: s in say + h in hat
combinations.t: Aspirated s — the s from 'say' followed by a puff of air (h from 'hat'). IPA: [sʰ].

combinations.s: sh in she + y in yet
combinations.t: Palatalised sh — the sh from 'she/show' followed by the y from 'yet'. IPA: [ʃʲ].

combinations.s: t in take + h in hat
combinations.t: Aspirated t — the t from 'take' followed by a puff of air (h). Contrast with plain t (unaspirated). IPA: [tʰ].

combinations.s: between j and ch + y in yet
combinations.t: Between j and ch, followed by y. The exact realisation varies by community and speaker. IPA: [dʒ~tʃʲ].

combinations.s: gee — between j and ch before vowels
combinations.t: Central dialect form — tsi between consonants, often pronounced 'gee'. Used at Six Nations/Tyendinaga. Eastern dialect writes ts instead. IPA: [dʒ].

combinations.s: ch in church + y in yet
combinations.t: Palatalised tsh — the ch from 'church' followed by the y from 'yet'. IPA: [tʃʲ].

combinations.s: dee-ye — d in dog + y in yet
combinations.t: Palatalised t/d — the d from 'dog' followed by the y from 'yet'. IPA: [dʲ].
Alternate spelling of hw. Pronounced as a soft f / voiceless w. Some writers use wh, others hw — both represent the same sound. IPA: [ʍ].
